agents.name: Goal Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Turn approved recommendations into scoped, testable execution briefs. Keep plans concrete, bounded, aligned with the outcome strategy, and focused on evidence that moves the Ultimate Goal checklist forward.

Turn the approved recommendation into a scoped goal for the next coding pass.

The output must represent one bounded task for a single cycle, not a broad multi-phase project.

The result must be executable by one coding agent in one pass. If related checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence, plan the largest coherent reviewable batch instead of narrowing to a tiny evidence-only slice.

Do not scope another coding pass just to re-prove a semantically identical checklist batch that already has direct evidence. If the approved recommendation is about checklist cleanup, focus the brief on the concrete missing evidence or redundant checks named in the recommendation.

`executionBrief` should be a full prompt for the coding agent: what to change, where to focus, what to avoid, and how to know the task is done.

Make acceptanceCriteria concrete and testable.

Keep acceptanceCriteria to at most 6 bullets and testStrategy to at most 4 focused checks.

Use the Goal checklist as the completion source of truth. Prefer a scoped plan that turns a coherent group of unmet or unknown required checks into met checks with evidence.

Keep constraints aligned with the Ultimate Goal and the repository boundaries.

Use the outcome strategy below to keep the scoped plan pointed at the best finished project outcome.

Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
- Completion source of truth: 2/78 required goal checks met (3%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.
- Highest-impact open checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview pr…; Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, sou…; Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice qu….
- Efficient batching opportunity: includes: The app includes a “speed concepts” game where I match concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples.; The app includes a “spot the flaw” game where I identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations.; The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithm…; The app includes a mini-game hub with fun, repeatable games that reinforce actual interview skills rather than unrelated gamification..
- Current blockers: none.
- Quality bar: The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice. Core functionalit…
- Preserve constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless…; If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistenc…; The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage conn…; Research can be performed by the coding agent during development, but the finished app should store useful structured local content and source notes….
- Avoid non-goals: Do not deploy the website publicly, configure production hosting, buy a domain, create user accounts, add payments, or package a mobile/desktop app u…; Do not build a social network, public leaderboard, multiplayer game, chat room, or community submission system as part of this goal.; Do not require integration with OpenAI, Anthropic, Reddit, YouTube, Twitter/X, Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, or any other external API for the finished app….

Use the relevant prior context below when it is directly applicable. Keep the scoped goal compact and do not include unrelated historical notes. Current checklist counts override historical progress counts in prior context.

Relevant prior context selected by deterministic similarity:
- Cycle 1 recommendation (score 0.51): Recommendation Agent | Recommendations ready | The repository is effectively empty: only Git metadata and an initial commit are present, with no app scaffold, content model, seed data, tests, or UI. The Ultimate Goal is not satisfied; the next cycle should establish the local-first app foundation while directly addressing the highest-impact company-guide checks. | The repository is effectively empty: only Git metadata and an initial commit are present, with no app scaffold, content model, seed data, tests, or UI...
  Why selected: shared work type: trading, ui, build; shared terms: the, and, app, local

Project: quant_interview_prep

Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

Detailed intent: The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles. It should combine a large structured question bank, company-specific interview guides, step-by-step answer explanations, interactive practice modes, mini-games, progress tracking, recruiting roadmaps, compensation research, and source-aware interview intelligence into one cohesive localhost website. Prioritize correctness, explanation depth, source traceability, repeat-practice value, content organization, and long-term maintainability over shallow visual flash, while still making the interface feel premium, modern, fast, and enjoyable to use. The system should impress a serious quant club member, quant researcher, trader, or technically strong recruiter by making quant recruiting preparation feel unified, rigorous, practical, and addictive enough to practice daily.

Approved recommendation: Satisfy goal batch: includes

Recommendation summary: Treat includes as the next SWE task group covering 17 related required checks.

Why now: These checks belong together by implementation path, validation evidence, or architecture. Representative checks: The app includes a “speed concepts” game where I match concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples.; The app includes a “spot….

Expected impact: This turns a coherent task group into repository evidence instead of spending cycles on isolated checklist items.

Ultimate-goal success criteria:
- The app provides a polished landing dashboard showing today’s recommended practice, weak topics, mastered topics, recent questions, progress streaks, upcoming recruiting focus areas, company targets, and quick links into timed drills, m...[truncated]
- The app includes major content sections for Quant Research, Quant Trading, Probability, Statistics, Expected Value, Brain Teasers, Mental Math, Market Making, Options and Derivatives, Coding, Python/Data Analysis, Machine Learning, Comp...[truncated]
- The app includes a structured question bank with questions, answers, hints, step-by-step explanations, tags, topic categories, difficulty levels, estimated time, interview round type, role relevance, firm associations, source notes, and mastery status.
- The app includes a substantial seed question bank covering probability, conditional probability, Bayes’ rule, expected value, variance, distributions, order statistics, Markov chains, stochastic processes, random walks, combinatorics, g...[truncated]
- The app includes Green Book-style probability and brain-teaser questions, but should create original/paraphrased variants and explanations rather than copying copyrighted book text verbatim.
- The app includes original practice questions created specifically for the platform, with realistic quant-interview style wording and detailed explanations that teach the reasoning process instead of only giving the final answer.
- The app includes curated questions inspired by public interview reports, public forums, public videos, public blogs, quant prep websites, Reddit discussions, YouTube videos, GitHub resources, and other accessible public materials, with...[truncated]
- The app includes a content model that distinguishes original questions, paraphrased public-report questions, user-created questions, Green Book-style questions, coding questions, company-reported questions, and compensation/recruiting notes.

Project constraints:
- If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless there is a clear reason to refactor.
- If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistence, unless the repository already indicates a different preferred framework.
- The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage connections, or external services at runtime.
- Research can be performed by the coding agent during development, but the finished app should store useful structured local content and source notes instead of depending on live web scraping every time it runs.
- Do not store API keys, tokens, personal credentials, private cookies, private database dumps, private interview databases, or machine-specific absolute paths in source code, committed files, logs, fixtures, or generated artifacts.
- Use only publicly available information, original generated questions, user-provided content, and clearly paraphrased summaries of public interview experiences.
- Do not bypass logins, paywalls, robots restrictions, terms of service, or access controls to collect questions or compensation data.
- Do not copy entire copyrighted prep books, paid-course content, proprietary interview databases, or long verbatim passages from websites, Reddit posts, videos, or books.

events.detail: {"summary":"Implement the next coherent checklist batch: a local-first mini-game hub with three repeatable quant interview skill games—Speed Concepts, Spot the Flaw, and Mental Math Speed—backed by structured local data, deterministic scoring logic, lightweight progress persistence, and reviewable tests/evidence.","executionBrief":"You are the coding agent for one bounded pass in `quant_interview_prep` . Focus only on turning the approved “includes” game-batch recommendation into direct repository evidence. First inspect the existing project and preserve its stack, scripts, routing, styling conventions, and architecture. If the repo is still effectively greenfield, scaffold only the minimum local-first React + TypeScript + Vite-style app needed to support this pass. Build a polished mini-game hub that links to three working games: (1) Speed Concepts, where users match quant concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples; (2) Spot the Flaw, where users identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations; and (3) Mental Math Speed, covering arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithmetic, and trading-relevant calculations. Keep all game content in structured local files with source/status metadata where applicable; original generated content is acceptable and preferred. Implement shared deterministic game/session logic for prompts, answer validation, scoring, streaks, timers/round limits, explanations, and progress summaries so the UI does not duplicate rules. Add lightweight local persistence for best scores/recent attempts if the app already has a persistence pattern; otherwise use safe browser localStorage behind a small utility. Ensure the hub feels useful for daily quant prep, not unrelated gamification: show skill targets, estimated time, recent/best score state, and quick start/review flows. Avoid external runtime APIs, auth, paid services, scraped/copyrighted question text, broad company-guide work, social/leaderboard features, or unrelated redesigns. The task is done when the three games are usable from localhost, backed by local structured content, tested at the core-logic level, and evidenced by passing project checks plus concise notes mapping the implemented UI/data/tests to the relevant mini-game checklist items.","acceptanceCriteria":["A mini-game hub route or section exists and presents Speed Concepts, Spot the Flaw, and Mental Math Speed as distinct repeatable interview-skill games with quick-start entry points and visible practice value.","Each of the three games is playable end-to-end locally: prompts render, answers can be submitted, correctness/scoring is shown, explanations or teaching feedback appear, and the user can start another round without reloading the app.","Game content is stored in structured local data with enough seeded items to demonstrate each required category: concept-definition/formula/use-case/example matching, flawed reasoning across quant/ML/trading topics, and mental math across arithmetic/fractions/percentages/approximations/probability/EV/trading calculations.","Shared game/session logic is separated from presentation and covered by deterministic tests for prompt selection, answer validation, scoring/streak behavior, round completion, and at least one edge case per game type.","Progress or attempt state is persisted locally or integrated into an existing progress model, with the hub showing recent/best score evidence without requiring accounts, credentials, or external services.","The implementation preserves the existing stack and passes the repository’s relevant lint/typecheck/test/build command set, or documents any unavailable script clearly in the final evidence."],"constraints":["Stay inside the active project root and do not inspect or depend on files outside `quant_interview_prep` .","Preserve the existing project stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless the repo is greenfield; agents.name: Recommendation Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Produce ranked next actions based on the repository state, the current cycle, unresolved issues, and the highest-impact unmet Ultimate Goal checks. Perform checklist governance first: add only necessary required checks, remove redundant or no-longer-applicable checks with evidence, and prefer coherent one-cycle batches that improve the finished project outcome with direct evidence.

Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.

First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.

Return 0 to 5 recommendations.

Every recommendation must be a concrete, single-cycle task. Prefer the largest coherent reviewable batch when related Goal checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence; split unrelated work apart.

Think like a small SWE team decomposing the Ultimate Goal into tasks. Checklist items are acceptance requirements; a cycle is a task that should cover the coherent subset of requirements that naturally belong together.

Prefer recommendations that are easy for a coding agent to execute and for an integrity agent to verify in one cycle, even when the recommendation covers a small batch of related checks.

When any required Goal checklist item is unmet or unknown, rank direct work on those checklist items ahead of generic stabilization, package-script cleanup, operator-feedback tweaks, or recently changed file follow-up unless there is an explicit open blocker.

For checklist work, recommend the option that can most directly move one coherent group of related required checks from unmet/unknown to met with repository evidence. Avoid claiming a feature check is satisfied through unrelated harness or package-only work.

Use the task map to reason about how many cycles the project probably needs. Do not choose a random fixed number of checklist items; choose the next logical task group based on shared implementation paths, tests, evidence, user value, and blocking order.

Default to medium scope for cohesive task groups, and small scope only for a genuinely isolated single-check task. Large scope should still be split into the next reviewable task group instead of flattened into item-by-item churn.

Do not recommend end-to-end milestones, phase-wide deliverables, or umbrella workflows that mix unrelated areas.

Do not plan to complete checklist items one by one when a coherent implementation or validation batch can cover related items. State the batch as one recommendation with the target checks named in the summary or rationale.

When a generated or improved interface needs live external data, recommend a provider abstraction with explicit offline demo/mock mode, live adapter mode when credentials are configured, and missing-credentials loading/error/empty states. Do not silently replace needed live data with local-only mock data.

For trading, brokerage, market-data, analytics, or finance interfaces, do not hardcode one provider unless the user chose it. If a free/no-card API key would unlock the live feature, recommend implementing the adapter and creating a credential request instead of settling for mock-only behavior; paid credentials require explicit operator choice.

Before ranking implementation work, perform a Goal checklist governance pass. Decide whether the current checklist is complete, missing an absolutely necessary required check, or carrying redundant/unnecessary checks that should no longer block completion.

Use goalCheckUpdates to keep the checklist adaptive: add missing required checks, update evidence/status, remove redundant checks, and consolidate near-duplicates. Prefer meaningful governance over artificial item limits.

If the current checklist is already large, do not add new required checks unless they are indispensable safety/security constraints. Consolidate redundant checks or mark no-longer-applicable checks instead.

Add a new required checklist item only when it is indispensable to the stated Ultimate Goal or an explicit safety/security constraint and is not already covered by an existing check. Do not add speculative nice-to-have work as required.

When two or more checklist items describe the same obligation, update the strongest existing item and include remove updates for redundant duplicates. For user-derived checks, remove means mark not_applicable with evidence rather than silently deleting the operator's intent.

If repository evidence shows a checklist item is unnecessary, impossible under the stated non-goals, or redundant with a more precise check, return a goalCheckUpdates entry with action remove, status not_applicable, and concrete rationale.

Maintain the Goal checklist. You may add a required check, mark a check unmet/unknown if evidence shows it is not actually done, mark a check met only when repository evidence or validation output supports it, or remove/not-applicable checks when they are redundant or no longer necessary.

Do not mark the Ultimate Goal satisfied unless every required Goal checklist item is met and no open blockers remain. Accepted decisions, completed cycles, and passing tests for a small slice are not enough by themselves.

Return goalCheckUpdates as an array every time, even when it is empty. Return one update for every checklist status you add or change. Each met check must include concrete evidence.

Estimate completion from required Goal checklist items only: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks.

If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.

Use only relative project paths in relatedPaths.

Use the deterministic outcome strategy below as the decision frame. It is guidance, not a license to ignore concrete repository evidence.

Outcome strategy:
- Ultimate target: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
- Completion source of truth: 2/77 required goal checks met (3%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.
- Highest-impact open checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview pr…; Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, sou…; Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice qu…; Each company page includes an “Interview Style” section explaining whether the firm tends to emphasize probability puzzles, mental math, market makin….
- Efficient batching opportunity: includes: The app includes a “speed concepts” game where I match concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples.; The app includes a “spot the flaw” game where I identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations.; The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithm…; The app includes a mini-game hub with fun, repeatable games that reinforce actual interview skills rather than unrelated gamification..
- Current blockers: none.
- Quality bar: The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice. Core functionalit…

- 12 additional task groups exist beyond this prompt excerpt; use the group counts, repository evidence, and checklist updates to keep the task plan adaptive.

Detailed intent: The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles. agents.name: Recommendation Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Produce ranked next actions based on the repository state, the current cycle, unresolved issues, and the highest-impact unmet Ultimate Goal checks. Perform checklist governance first: add only necessary required checks, remove redundant or no-longer-applicable checks with evidence, and prefer coherent one-cycle batches that improve the finished project outcome with direct evidence.

Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.

First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.

Return 0 to 5 recommendations.

Every recommendation must be a concrete, single-cycle task. Prefer the largest coherent reviewable batch when related Goal checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence; split unrelated work apart.

Think like a small SWE team decomposing the Ultimate Goal into tasks. Checklist items are acceptance requirements; a cycle is a task that should cover the coherent subset of requirements that naturally belong together.

Prefer recommendations that are easy for a coding agent to execute and for an integrity agent to verify in one cycle, even when the recommendation covers a small batch of related checks.

When any required Goal checklist item is unmet or unknown, rank direct work on those checklist items ahead of generic stabilization, package-script cleanup, operator-feedback tweaks, or recently changed file follow-up unless there is an explicit open blocker.

For checklist work, recommend the option that can most directly move one coherent group of related required checks from unmet/unknown to met with repository evidence. Avoid claiming a feature check is satisfied through unrelated harness or package-only work.

Use the task map to reason about how many cycles the project probably needs. Do not choose a random fixed number of checklist items; choose the next logical task group based on shared implementation paths, tests, evidence, user value, and blocking order.

Default to medium scope for cohesive task groups, and small scope only for a genuinely isolated single-check task. Large scope should still be split into the next reviewable task group instead of flattened into item-by-item churn.

Do not recommend end-to-end milestones, phase-wide deliverables, or umbrella workflows that mix unrelated areas.

Do not plan to complete checklist items one by one when a coherent implementation or validation batch can cover related items. State the batch as one recommendation with the target checks named in the summary or rationale.

When a generated or improved interface needs live external data, recommend a provider abstraction with explicit offline demo/mock mode, live adapter mode when credentials are configured, and missing-credentials loading/error/empty states. Do not silently replace needed live data with local-only mock data.

For trading, brokerage, market-data, analytics, or finance interfaces, do not hardcode one provider unless the user chose it. If a free/no-card API key would unlock the live feature, recommend implementing the adapter and creating a credential request instead of settling for mock-only behavior; paid credentials require explicit operator choice.

Before ranking implementation work, perform a Goal checklist governance pass. Decide whether the current checklist is complete, missing an absolutely necessary required check, or carrying redundant/unnecessary checks that should no longer block completion.

Use goalCheckUpdates to keep the checklist adaptive: add missing required checks, update evidence/status, remove redundant checks, and consolidate near-duplicates. Prefer meaningful governance over artificial item limits.

If the current checklist is already large, do not add new required checks unless they are indispensable safety/security constraints. Consolidate redundant checks or mark no-longer-applicable checks instead.

Add a new required checklist item only when it is indispensable to the stated Ultimate Goal or an explicit safety/security constraint and is not already covered by an existing check. Do not add speculative nice-to-have work as required.

When two or more checklist items describe the same obligation, update the strongest existing item and include remove updates for redundant duplicates. For user-derived checks, remove means mark not_applicable with evidence rather than silently deleting the operator's intent.

If repository evidence shows a checklist item is unnecessary, impossible under the stated non-goals, or redundant with a more precise check, return a goalCheckUpdates entry with action remove, status not_applicable, and concrete rationale.

Maintain the Goal checklist. You may add a required check, mark a check unmet/unknown if evidence shows it is not actually done, mark a check met only when repository evidence or validation output supports it, or remove/not-applicable checks when they are redundant or no longer necessary.

Do not mark the Ultimate Goal satisfied unless every required Goal checklist item is met and no open blockers remain. Accepted decisions, completed cycles, and passing tests for a small slice are not enough by themselves.

Return goalCheckUpdates as an array every time, even when it is empty. Return one update for every checklist status you add or change. Each met check must include concrete evidence.

Estimate completion from required Goal checklist items only: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks.

If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.

Use only relative project paths in relatedPaths.

Use the deterministic outcome strategy below as the decision frame. It is guidance, not a license to ignore concrete repository evidence.

- Completion source of truth: 2/77 required goal checks met (3%).
- Primary next move: Satisfy the highest-impact coherent batch of related goal checks with direct repository evidence.

- company: 3 open required checks; representative checks: Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and…; Each company page includes an “Interview Style” section explaining whether the firm tends to emphasize probability puzzles, mental math, market makin…; The app includes company guide pages accessible from a visually polished company directory with cards, logos or text placeholders, role tags, difficu….

Project: quant_interview_prep

Project kind: git

Workflow objective: Stop once the stated Ultimate Goal is satisfied.

Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.

Detailed intent: The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles. It should combine a large structured question bank, company-specific interview guides, step-by-step answer explanations, interactive practice modes, mini-games, progress tracking, recruiting roadmaps, compensation research, and source-aware interview intelligence into one cohesive localhost website. Prioritize correctness, explanation depth, source traceability, repeat-practice value, content organization, and long-term maintainability over shallow visual flash, while still making the interface feel premium, modern, fast, and enjoyable to use. The system should impress a serious quant club member, quant researcher, trader, or technically strong recruiter by making quant recruiting preparation feel unified, rigorous, practical, and addictive enough to practice daily.

Repository overview: quant_interview_prep is effectively empty and ready for setup.

Important to know first: Skip repository analysis for now and define the Ultimate Goal or setup plan first.

agents.name: Recommendation Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Produce ranked next actions based on the repository state, the current cycle, unresolved issues, and the highest-impact unmet Ultimate Goal checks. Perform checklist governance first: add only necessary required checks, remove redundant or no-longer-applicable checks with evidence, and prefer coherent one-cycle batches that improve the finished project outcome with direct evidence.

Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.

First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.

Return 0 to 5 recommendations.

Every recommendation must be a concrete, single-cycle task. Prefer the largest coherent reviewable batch when related Goal checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence; split unrelated work apart.

Prefer recommendations that are easy for a coding agent to execute and for an integrity agent to verify in one cycle, even when the recommendation covers a small batch of related checks.

When any required Goal checklist item is unmet or unknown, rank direct work on those checklist items ahead of generic stabilization, package-script cleanup, operator-feedback tweaks, or recently changed file follow-up unless there is an explicit open blocker.

For checklist work, recommend the option that can most directly move one coherent group of related required checks from unmet/unknown to met with repository evidence. Avoid claiming a feature check is satisfied through unrelated harness or package-only work.

Default to medium scope for cohesive batches of 2-4 related checks, and small scope for a true single-check task. Large scope should still be avoided.

Do not recommend end-to-end milestones, phase-wide deliverables, or umbrella workflows that mix unrelated areas.

When a generated or improved interface needs live external data, recommend a provider abstraction with explicit offline demo/mock mode, live adapter mode when credentials are configured, and missing-credentials loading/error/empty states. Do not silently replace needed live data with local-only mock data.

For trading, brokerage, market-data, analytics, or finance interfaces, do not hardcode one provider unless the user chose it. If a free/no-card API key would unlock the live feature, recommend implementing the adapter and creating a credential request instead of settling for mock-only behavior; paid credentials require explicit operator choice.

Before ranking implementation work, perform a Goal checklist governance pass. Decide whether the current checklist is complete, missing an absolutely necessary required check, or carrying redundant/unnecessary checks that should no longer block completion.

Add a new required checklist item only when it is indispensable to the stated Ultimate Goal or an explicit safety/security constraint and is not already covered by an existing check. Do not add speculative nice-to-have work as required.

When two or more checklist items describe the same obligation, update the strongest existing item and include remove updates for redundant duplicates. For user-derived checks, remove means mark not_applicable with evidence rather than silently deleting the operator's intent.

If repository evidence shows a checklist item is unnecessary, impossible under the stated non-goals, or redundant with a more precise check, return a goalCheckUpdates entry with action remove, status not_applicable, and concrete rationale.

Maintain the Goal checklist. You may add a required check, mark a check unmet/unknown if evidence shows it is not actually done, mark a check met only when repository evidence or validation output supports it, or remove/not-applicable checks when they are redundant or no longer necessary.

Do not mark the Ultimate Goal satisfied unless every required Goal checklist item is met and no open blockers remain. Accepted decisions, completed cycles, and passing tests for a small slice are not enough by themselves.

Return goalCheckUpdates as an array every time, even when it is empty. Return one update for every checklist status you add or change. Each met check must include concrete evidence.

Estimate completion from required Goal checklist items only: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks.

If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.

Use only relative project paths in relatedPaths.

Use the deterministic outcome strategy below as the decision frame. It is guidance, not a license to ignore concrete repository evidence.

timeline.detail: {"summary":"Implement the next coherent checklist batch: a local-first mini-game hub with three repeatable quant interview skill games—Speed Concepts, Spot the Flaw, and Mental Math Speed—backed by structured local data, deterministic scoring logic, lightweight progress persistence, and reviewable tests/evidence.","executionBrief":"You are the coding agent for one bounded pass in `quant_interview_prep` . Focus only on turning the approved “includes” game-batch recommendation into direct repository evidence. First inspect the existing project and preserve its stack, scripts, routing, styling conventions, and architecture. If the repo is still effectively greenfield, scaffold only the minimum local-first React + TypeScript + Vite-style app needed to support this pass. Build a polished mini-game hub that links to three working games: (1) Speed Concepts, where users match quant concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples; (2) Spot the Flaw, where users identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations; and (3) Mental Math Speed, covering arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithmetic, and trading-relevant calculations. Keep all game content in structured local files with source/status metadata where applicable; original generated content is acceptable and preferred. Implement shared deterministic game/session logic for prompts, answer validation, scoring, streaks, timers/round limits, explanations, and progress summaries so the UI does not duplicate rules. Add lightweight local persistence for best scores/recent attempts if the app already has a persistence pattern; otherwise use safe browser localStorage behind a small utility. Ensure the hub feels useful for daily quant prep, not unrelated gamification: show skill targets, estimated time, recent/best score state, and quick start/review flows. Avoid external runtime APIs, auth, paid services, scraped/copyrighted question text, broad company-guide work, social/leaderboard features, or unrelated redesigns. The task is done when the three games are usable from localhost, backed by local structured content, tested at the core-logic level, and evidenced by passing project checks plus concise notes mapping the implemented UI/data/tests to the relevant mini-game checklist items.","acceptanceCriteria":["A mini-game hub route or section exists and presents Speed Concepts, Spot the Flaw, and Mental Math Speed as distinct repeatable interview-skill games with quick-start entry points and visible practice value.","Each of the three games is playable end-to-end locally: prompts render, answers can be submitted, correctness/scoring is shown, explanations or teaching feedback appear, and the user can start another round without reloading the app.","Game content is stored in structured local data with enough seeded items to demonstrate each required category: concept-definition/formula/use-case/example matching, flawed reasoning across quant/ML/trading topics, and mental math across arithmetic/fractions/percentages/approximations/probability/EV/trading calculations.","Shared game/session logic is separated from presentation and covered by deterministic tests for prompt selection, answer validation, scoring/streak behavior, round completion, and at least one edge case per game type.","Progress or attempt state is persisted locally or integrated into an existing progress model, with the hub showing recent/best score evidence without requiring accounts, credentials, or external services.","The implementation preserves the existing stack and passes the repository’s relevant lint/typecheck/test/build command set, or documents any unavailable script clearly in the final evidence."],"constraints":["Stay inside the active project root and do not inspect or depend on files outside `quant_interview_prep` .","Preserve the existing project stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless the repo is greenfield; if greenfield, keep scaffolding minimal and local-first.","The finished app must run on localhost without paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage connections, or external services at runtime.","Use original/generated educational content or clearly paraphrased public-style examples; do not copy copyrighted prep books, paid-course content, proprietary databases, or long verbatim web/forum passages.","Keep a clean separation between local content data, source/status metadata, game logic, progress persistence, and UI rendering.","Do not expand this pass into company pages, compensation research, production hosting, social features, public leaderboards, or unrelated app-wide redesigns."],"testStrategy":["Run the repository’s standard install-free checks where available, prioritizing typecheck, unit tests, lint, and production build.","Add or update unit tests for shared game logic and each game mode’s validation/scoring behavior using local fixtures only.","Manually smoke-test localhost navigation from the hub into all three games, completing at least one round per game and confirming feedback plus replay behavior.","Verify local persistence by completing a round, refreshing, and confirming recent/best score state appears without network credentials or external API calls."]}

timeline.title: Agent message
timeline.detail: Implemented the greenfield mini-game batch as a local-first React + TypeScript + Vite app. What changed: - Scaffolded app/runtime: - `package.json` , `vite.config.ts` , `tsconfig*.json` , `index.html` , `.gitignore` - `src/main.tsx` , `src/App.tsx` , `src/styles/global.css` - Added structured local game content: - `src/data/speedConcepts.ts` — definitions, formulas, use cases, interview examples - `src/data/spotTheFlaw.ts` — probability, statistics, ML, trading reasoning flaws - `src/data/mentalMath.ts` — arithmetic, fractions, percentages, approximations/logs, probability, EV, trading calculations - Source/status metadata via `src/data/sourceMetadata.ts` - Added shared game logic: - `src/lib/gameEngine.ts` for deterministic prompt queues, answer validation, scoring, streaks, timers, round completion, explanations, summaries, choice shuffling - `src/lib/progressStorage.ts` for safe browser `localStorage` recent/best attempt persistence - Added tests and smoke evidence: - `src/lib/gameEngine.test.ts` with 11 deterministic core-logic tests - `smoke/mini-games.spec.ts` covering localhost hub navigation, all three playable games, replay, feedback, and persistence after refresh Implemented checklist mapping: - Mini-game hub: shows all three games with skill targets, estimated time, local prompt count, recent/best score state, quick start/review actions. - Speed Concepts: playable matching game across definition/formula/use-case/interview-example content. - Spot the Flaw: playable flaw-identification game across probability/statistics/ML/trading. - Mental Math Speed: playable numeric speed drill across all requested math categories. - Shared logic separated from UI and tested. - Local progress persists without auth, APIs, credentials, or network runtime dependencies. Verification run: - `npm run typecheck` ✅ - `npm run test` ✅ 11 tests passed - `npm run build` ✅ - `npm run lint` ✅ - `npm audit --audit-level=high` ✅ 0 vulnerabilities - `PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH=0 npx playwright test smoke/mini-games.spec.ts --browser=chromium` ✅ 1 smoke test passed Notes: - The repo was effectively greenfield, so I scaffolded the minimal local-first Vite/React/TS app. - No external runtime APIs, auth, credentials, leaderboards, or scraped/copyrighted content were added.

timeline.title: Agent message
timeline.detail: Implemented the cohesive mini-game batch. Changed: - `src/App.tsx` , `src/styles/global.css` - Polished hub cards with topic coverage, difficulty mix, interview-transfer notes, local-only/privacy note, aggregate progress, total score, modes tried, best streak. - Added game-page round context: local seed, deck coverage, debrief prompt, interview transfer. - Added deterministic local round seeds and “Replay this seed” support. - `src/data/games.ts` , `src/lib/gameTypes.ts` - Added structured `interviewTransfer` , `roundGoal` , and `debriefPrompt` metadata for each mode. - `src/lib/gameEngine.ts` , `src/lib/progressStorage.ts` - Sessions now store the local seed; completed attempts persist it. - Choice shuffling now uses the deterministic session seed instead of timestamp-derived session id. - `src/lib/gameEngine.test.ts` - Added tests proving required deck coverage: - Speed Concepts: definition/formula/use-case/interview-example. - Spot the Flaw: probability/statistics/ML/trading. - Mental Math: arithmetic/fractions/percentages/log approximations/probability arithmetic/EV. - Added seed determinism and metadata integrity checks. - `smoke/mini-games.spec.ts` , `playwright.config.ts` , `package.json` - Added configured `npm run test:smoke` Playwright flow with Vite web server. - Smoke now checks hub metadata, all three game pages, interactions, feedback, summary, replay, and persistence. Verification: - `npm run test` ✅ 16 tests passed. - `npm run build` ✅ production build passed. - `npm run lint` ✅ TypeScript check passed. - `npx playwright test --list` ✅ smoke test discovered. - `npm run test:smoke` ⚠️ blocked by missing Playwright Chromium binary in this environment, not by app/test code: - Missing executable under `/home/nicot/.cache/ms-playwright/...` - I did not install browsers because that would write outside the active project boundary. No external APIs, credentials, scraping, or secrets were added.
